transcript: Trees and shrubs can be planted any time of the year as long as you can work the soil, but the best times to plant are in fall and late winter and early spring. But no matter when you plant, there are some important steps that you need to take to set those trees and shrubs up for success, and that's what we're going to cover today. And as we do, a quick shout out to Exmark, the makers of professional mowing equipment, for sponsoring this video. Setting your trees up for success means siding them properly in the landscape for their preferred growing environment. So when in doubt, just check the tag on the tree or shrub and that will tell you if it's a full sun or maybe a partial shade condition. But make sure that you get those plants right where they need to be. Once I've picked out that ideal spot, I like to use the plant or the tree in position to figure out how big or wide I'm going to dig that hole. So I'll just basically pull the shovel out two to three times the width of the container. You want that hole to be extra wide but no deeper than the plant was growing in the container. So I'll just use the tip of the shovel and draw out a little bit so that I know where my hole is going to be. Okay that's two to three times the width and now I'll get this out of the way and I'll start to dig my hole. Okay so I think I have my hole about the size that I want it but the way that you know for sure is to bring the plant over to the hole, and you're checking for the width and you're checking for the depth. Now the width, that's about right, and as far as the depth, I want to make sure that the level that this plant was growing in the container is no deeper than the hole. The way you check this, I like to take the handle of my shovel, lay it across the grade, and then you have a template to know and you can compare that way. And so I'll just take this out of the hole right now while I can and add a little more soil to the hole to build up that place right underneath the root ball. If I don't do it now I'll never do it and this is the time. Plus you get some settling anyway so it's better to play it safe right now while you can. That's better but I like how that looks. Now the next step is to check the roots and this is the only opportunity you have to do this what you're looking for here is to make sure that the roots aren't constricted all around in a circular pattern that's called root bound and you don't want that this actually looks pretty good these roots have just started to reach the outer edge and they don't have a tight circular pattern yet. And that's good because if they did, you would need to break this up. I still like to run my fingers through this root ball a little bit. I call it liberating the roots and that will redirect them to grow out into the native soil rather than stay in that circular pattern. Because if that's what you find when you take the root ball out of the container, it's really important that you do this step right here to break up those roots. Otherwise they'll continue in that pattern and they'll never really reach their full potential because those roots will never grow out into the surrounding soil. Okay the roots are nice and liberated, the hole is wide enough, it's not too deep, and now it's ready for the backfill. Now, let me bust a myth at this point. A lot of people think, and rightfully so, that it makes sense when they're adding back soil that they add back soil that's better than the soil that they took out, but that's a myth. You don't want to improve the soil going back in because if you make this environment too good relative to the native soil the roots just stay in that really plush area and they don't explore the native soil and you want them to do that and the best way to do that and studies prove this is to just backfill with the soil that you took out of the hole. Now you can take out the rocks and you can knock down the clumps, but just put back the soil that you took out. That's the best way to go. And don't feel the need to use it all, but I like to use it all. I'll just taper it away from the base. And that's also good because as it rains or as you water, it allows the water to run off. And this is one of the reasons that we plant this a little bit higher whether it's a tree or a shrub we don't want to be too deep because when we water that way if the soil is too heavy the water is just going to rush towards the center and potentially sit in the basin and it can drown the tree or shrub and we don't want that of course so going higher allows that water to run away from the center and out towards the edge and that's actually better. Okay, so you see how that's tapered away from the base of the plant out towards the edges? That's what we're looking for. So we've backfilled with the native soil. Now the next step is to water it in and knock out those air pockets and give those roots a good drink of water. Now don't go really hard and heavy at this point. I have a water breaker on the end of this hose that just breaks up the flow a little bit but I'll just sit here and make sure that I get all the soil good and saturated so that I know the roots are getting plenty of water at this point. This is a really important step in this process is to get plenty of water down in there so the roots have a chance to just take it up. This is kind of a shocking move for them at this point and so the water really helps them recover through this period. Now one thing you could do here is just put it on a really low volume like barely a trickle like that and just set it at the base of the plant and walk away for an hour two hours three hours you're not going to hurt the plant at this point but you're really going to give an opportunity for saturation of the soil at this point this is really the only time you want to be that wet after that just periodically come back every other day or so until this plant is established now how long can that take a couple weeks a couple months maybe even longer depending on the temperature and the amount of supplemental rain that you're getting but to check that soil to know that it's moist enough just stick your finger into the soil and if your finger comes up with it being dirty like this then the soil is moist enough but if you put your finger down in there and brought it back up and it was relatively clean the soil is too dry and it's time to water again but then keep an eye on the plant and look for signs of leaf drop or yellowing or just limp leaves and that's also an indication that you might need to come in here and water but the best test that I I know of is called the finger test, and it's what I just showed you. Okay, so let's assume that we've got enough water into the root ball at this point. The soil is nice and moist, and now the last thing that I'm going to do is add some mulch. And mulch can be anything that's organic, so I love to use shredded leaves. Now the mulch is really important for a number of reasons. The main thing when it comes to setting your trees and shrubs up for success is that it helps hold that moisture in the soil. That is hugely important. It helps keep that soil warmer in the wintertime and cooler in the summertime. It keeps some of the weeds from germinating around the plant and therefore reduces the competition. You don't want that mulch is just important for a lot of reasons so use about a two inch layer all the way out to the drip line and where's the drip line well that's the farthest point on the tree or the shrub where those last leaves are basically the edge and mulch out at least that far and more is better but as far as how much mulch to get close to the base of the plant you actually don't want to go up right against it you want to give about a two inch space around it and push the mulch away from there like a donut and that prevents potential pests and diseases from finding their way up against the the stem or the base of the plant and so I've got that right just like that so. So I've got enough mulch. I've got about a two inch depth, which is what I'm going for. And the last thing I'm gonna do, now this is an optional step for you, but I like to use an automatic timer, basically a battery operated timer. And I put it on the end of my hose. And then I set that timer to come on every other day for about 30 minutes and that way I've got my watering on autopilot and I know that it's going to establish but I'll come out and check it to be sure that everything still looks good but this way if I have a business trip or vacation or something else I don't have to worry about the fact that my plants may not get enough water so an investment of about $35 in an automatic timer like this is well worth it for the health of your plants. Thanks for watching today's video and be sure to click on the link below to access X Marks Backyard Life website where you can watch more videos from me as well as other useful rural lifestyle outdoor cooking living, and lawn and garden content.
